title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Spot Prices Steadily Rising Amidst the Much-Awaited 8th November Workshop
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(30th September â€“ 06th October)
  • A total of 487,179 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 10.1% decline week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a 1.86x times rise as 2,778,703 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price reachedÂ $62.72/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 1.68% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 4.55% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price continues to remain above the $60 mark, rising 0.12% WoW; on 08th Oct, it was $68.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 1.14% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each increased by 2.99% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The LCFS market continues to gain momentum, as reflected in the recent rise in average LCFS spot credit prices, driven by expectations of a tightening market. CARB has released updates in its Second 15-Day Notice, published on 1st October, 2024, which incorporate stakeholder feedback and refine several previously proposed amendments to the LCFS program. Among the key changes, CARB has extended the deadline for hydrogen produced from fossil gas to be eligible for LCFS credits to 2035, requiring 80% renewable content by 2030. Additionally, the 20% credit limitation for virgin crop-based biofuels has been expanded to include sunflower oil, and quarterly assessments will now determine whether the Automatic Acceleration Mechanism (AAM) is triggered to adjust annual carbon intensity benchmarks. Other notable updates include an increase in the creditable capacity for public hydrogen refueling stations to 100%, and adjustments to the RNG crediting periods and book-and-claim accounting provisions. These changes are analysed by cCarbon in detail here. CARBâ€™s ongoing updates to the LCFS program are set to take effect in Q1 2025, with a public comment period open until 16th October, 2024, and a hearing scheduled for 8th November, 2024. California lawmakers' efforts to introduce a bill that would allow the sale of E15 gasoline, a 15% ethanol blend, appear to have stalled in the state Senate. The bill, ABX2-9, sponsored by Assemblywoman Cottie Petrie-Norris, aimed to address high consumer fuel prices by mandating the California Air Resources Board (CARB) to evaluate the transition from E10 to E15 ethanol-blended gasoline. While the bill passed the state Assembly without opposition, it has not progressed in the Senate, with Senate President Pro Tem Mike McGuire citing the need for further analysis. Environmental concerns, particularly regarding potential increases in NOx emissions, have contributed to California's long-standing ban on E15 gasoline. Despite the bill's stalling, the legislature is prioritizing AB X2-1, a separate proposal requiring in-state refiners to maintain minimum stocks of gasoline and blending components, which is supported by Governor Gavin Newsom. The special legislative session aims to address fuel price spikes, but the focus has now shifted away from the ethanol blend proposal.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_588550" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.68% WoW fall from $63.79 to $62.72 last week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices fell by 4.55% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 487,179 credits exchanged, recording a 10.1% WoW fall this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had increased by 1.86x times WoW this week, to reach 2,778,703 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_588551" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 0.12% WoW, reaching $66.13/MT. On 08th Oct, the average spot price was $68.00/MT, which significantly rose from $56.58/MT, the lowest point in September. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $68.85/MT as of 08th Oct, up from $57.67/MT as of 10thÂ Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_588552" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 158 trades were reported in the market, with 24 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $174.00 for 12,500 credits of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $40.50 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 250,000 credits in Type 2 were undertaken for a price of $50.86, and the smallest volume transfers of 3 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for a price of $64.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588553" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In August, trading activity experienced a significant downturn, with volumes declining sharply to 1.90 MMT from the robust 5.21 MMT recorded in July. Despite this contraction, a recovery was observed in September as volumes rebounded to 3.00 MMT. Notably, Type 1 prices, which had been on a downward trajectory until June, began to trend upwards and maintained this positive momentum through September. By the end of that month, the average spot price for Type 1 reached approximately $60.23, marking a sustained price recovery.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588554" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 30th Â Sep â€“ 06th Oct, total VPT recorded a 35.85% fall (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 1.14% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 2.99% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • U.S. Rep. Angie Craig Leads Bipartisan Bill to Make Year-Round E15 Permanent. U.S. Representative Angie Craig introduced bipartisan legislation to make year-round access to E15 permanent nationwide â€“ expanding market access for Minnesota farmers and lowering costs for drivers at the gas pump. The bipartisan Nationwide Consumer and Fuel Retailer Choice Act is the latest step in Rep. Craigâ€™s efforts to increase investment in domestic biofuels production. In Congress, Rep. Craig has worked across the aisle for years to promote homegrown biofuels and stood up to the Biden Administration when they missed the mark on new sustainable aviation fuel guidance earlier this year.
  • Avinaâ€™s Southern California Clean Hydrogen Project Hits Major Milestone. Avina Clean Hydrogen is thrilled to announce that it has received the industrial new construction permit to proceed with the construction of its integrated hydrogen production and refueling facility in Southern California. Once operational, the facility will produce 1,400 metric tons of compressed clean hydrogen per annum, enough to fuel nearly 100 fuel cell heavy-duty trucks and buses daily. With on-site dispensing systems, the facility will eliminate the logistical challenges that currently inflate hydrogen costs.
  • California Assembly Passes Bill Bolstering the Sale of E15 Gasoline Blends. On October 1st, the California State Assembly passed Bill ABX2-9, permitting the sale of E15 gasoline blends within the State. The Bill will now await approval and consideration in the Californian Senate. The move is part of an effort to support gasoline production in the State as promoted by the 2024 CEC Transportation Fuels Assessment. The Bill was introduced for consideration by Assemblywoman Cottie Petri-Norris. The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is expected to present solutions to bolster gasoline production in the State by 1st July 2025. The Bill proposes the application of a fee by the CARB on the production of E10 to E15, which would be capped at a total of $5,000,000 for a period of three years.
  • Second Public Availability of Modified Text and Availability of Additional Documents and/or Information for the Proposed Low Carbon Fuel Standard Amendments. On August 12, 2024, CARB issued a "Notice of Public Availability of Modified Text and Availability of Additional Documents and/or Information for the Proposed Low Carbon Fuel Standard Amendments" (First 15-Day Changes). The comment period for the First 15-Day Changes opened on August 12, 2024, and closed on August 27, 2024. CARB has determined that additional modifications are appropriate for the proposed amendments and has developed the proposed modifications as stated in the â€œSummary of Proposed Regulatory Modificationsâ€ section of the Second 15-Day Notice.
  • Ameresco, Republic Services and PG&E Celebrate the Opening of Californiaâ€™s Largest Landfill Gas to Renewable Natural Gas Plant. Ameresco, Inc.,Â  a leading cleantech integrator specializing in energy efficiency and renewable energy, Republic Services, Inc. and Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E) , celebrated the ribbon cutting for Californiaâ€™s largest and most resilient landfill gas (LFG) to renewable natural gas (RNG) plant, located at the Keller Canyon Landfill in Pittsburg, CA.This first of its kind energy center in the U.S. will be powered by a uniquely designed co-located LFG-to-Electric Plant and will serve as a major milestone in renewable energy production, converting landfill gas into clean, renewable natural gas.
